sources.notes: Read the operation, edge cases, and constraints: 2 <= n <= 200000 and sum n <= 200000.

Official editorial identifies the degree-plus-adjacency objective; its embedded accepted implementation checks non-neighbors by global degrees and neighbors with a one-point penalty, and it explicitly notes tree DP as an alternative.
Consulted full O(n log n) multiset solution that temporarily decreases every neighbor degree after fixing the first removed vertex.
Independent implementation cross-check for the ordered-multiset update/restore method and its O(n log n) bound.

annotation.summary: Delete exactly two distinct vertices from a tree and maximize the number of connected components in the remaining forest.
annotation.constraints: There are up to 10000 tests, 2 <= n <= 200000 per test, and the sum of n is at most 200000, so quadratic pair enumeration is impossible.
annotation.core_idea: For distinct u and v, the remaining component count is deg(u)+deg(v)-1 if they are nonadjacent and deg(u)+deg(v)-2 if they are adjacent. Fix u and define the second-vertex score as deg(v)-[uv is an edge]. Remove deg(u) from an ordered multiset, replace each neighbor degree deg(v) by deg(v)-1, query the maximum score, then restore all changes. This absorbs the adjacency case into one uniform maximum query.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory per test case.
tricks: Express the answer solely through the two degrees and one adjacency penalty.
Temporarily decrement all neighbors so adjacent and nonadjacent second choices share one maximum query.
Each adjacency-list entry is updated only when its endpoint is fixed, for O(n) total multiset changes.

solution_variants.name: enumerate-first-delete-ordered-multiset
solution_variants.summary: Enumerate u, remove its own degree, decrement neighbor keys in a global ordered multiset, and combine deg(u) with the largest remaining adjusted key.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n) time and O(n) memory

solution_variants.name: linear-postorder-pair-dp
solution_variants.summary: Root the tree and postorder-compute the maximum degree in each subtree and among proper descendants. Check adjacent parent-child pairs with penalty 1, u against descendants at distance at least two, and vertices from two different child subtrees; recursion handles pairs internal to one child subtree.

tags.evidence: The exact component formula follows because deleting vertices from a tree leaves a forest and the only overlap between their incident-edge losses is the edge uv.
For every fixed u, the main solution deletes and reinserts duplicate degree keys and queries the maximum adjusted degree of the second vertex.
The first removed vertex is exhaustively enumerated, while all second choices are collapsed into one maximum query.
Fixing u changes only each neighbor's second-vertex contribution by -1, and those local score changes are applied and rolled back.
The distinct linear alternative combines subtree maxima in postorder and classifies the optimal pair by its rooted-tree relationship.
Taking the maximum adjusted second vertex after u is fixed is a data-structure query, not an independent irrevocable greedy process or exchange argument.
